content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format

1→# AI Daemon
     2→
     3→A lightweight AI framework built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. Connect any LLM provider, extend capabilities with skills and MCPs, schedule autonomous jobs, and interact through REST, Web UI, or terminal -- all from a single self-hosted service.
     4→
     5→![Example use case](images/example.png)
     6→
     7→## Architecture
     8→
     9→```mermaid
    10→graph TB
    11→    User([User])
    12→
    13→    subgraph Interface["Interface Layer"]
    14→        REST[REST API]
    15→        WebUI[Web UI]
    16→        CLI[Interactive CLI]
    17→    end
    18→
    19→    User -->|HTTP / curl| REST
    20→    User -->|Browser| WebUI
    21→    User -->|Terminal| CLI
    22→    WebUI --> REST
    23→
    24→    subgraph Engine["AI Engine"]
    25→        Chat[Chat Service]
    26→        Conv[Conversation Manager]
    27→        Providers[Provider Registry]
    28→        Memory[Persistent Memory]
    29→        Skills[Skills Reader]
    30→        Shell[Shell Executor]
    31→        Scheduler[Job Scheduler]
    32→        MCPTools[MCP Tools]
    33→    end
    34→
    35→    subgraph Providers_Sub["LLM Providers"]
    36→        OpenAI[OpenAI]
    37→        Anthropic[Anthropic]
    38→        Gemini[Gemini]
    39→        Ollama[Ollama / Local]
    40→    end
    41→
    42→    subgraph External["External Integrations"]
    43→        MCPRemote[Remote MCPs]
    44→        MCPLocal[Local MCPs - stdio]
    45→        Smithery[Smithery Skills Registry]
    46→    end
    47→
    48→    subgraph Storage["~/.aidaemon/"]
    49→        ProvidersJSON[providers.json]
    50→        MemoryJSON[memory.json]
    51→        JobsJSON[jobs.json]
    52→        ConversationsDir[conversations/]
    53→        SkillsDir[skills/]
    54→        McpsDir[mcps/]
    55→    end
    56→
    57→    REST --> Chat
    58→    REST --> Conv
    59→    CLI --> Conv
    60→
    61→    Conv -->|multi-turn history| Chat
    62→    Chat --> Providers
    63→    Providers --> OpenAI & Anthropic & Gemini & Ollama
    64→
    65→    Chat -->|tool calls| Memory & Skills & Shell & Scheduler & MCPTools
    66→
    67→    Memory -->|read/write| MemoryJSON
    68→    Skills -->|read| SkillsDir
    69→    Scheduler -->|persist| JobsJSON
    70→    Scheduler -->|executes via| Chat
    71→    Conv -->|persist| ConversationsDir
    72→    MCPTools --> MCPRemote & MCPLocal
    73→    MCPRemote & MCPLocal -.->|config| McpsDir
    74→    Smithery -->|install all files| SkillsDir
    75→    Providers -.->|persist| ProvidersJSON
    76→
    77→    Shell -->|guarded by flag| OS[Host OS]
    78→
    79→    style User fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    80→    style Storage fill:#c9a800,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    81→    style External fill:#1a7a7a,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    82→```
    83→
    84→### Delegation
    85→
    86→When delegation is enabled, the main agent can spawn sub-agents to handle subtasks in parallel. Sub-agents run asynchronously; when they finish, the parent is woken with a status update and can synthesize results or send revision work.
    87→Sub-agents can create their own subagents. The diagram shows one parent and only top subagents for simplicitly.
    88→
    89→```mermaid
    90→sequenceDiagram
    91→    participant User
    92→    participant Parent as Parent Agent
    93→    participant DelegationService
    94→    participant Sub1 as Sub-agent 1
    95→    participant Sub2 as Sub-agent 2
    96→
    97→    User->>Parent: Message
    98→    Parent->>Parent: Estimates time > threshold
    99→    Parent->>DelegationService: delegateToSubAgent x2
   100→    DelegationService->>Sub1: Create and run sub-conversation
   101→    DelegationService->>Sub2: Create and run sub-conversation
   102→    Parent->>User: Delegated, will respond when ready
   103→    Sub1->>DelegationService: Completed
   104→    Sub2->>DelegationService: Completed
   105→    DelegationService->>Parent: Delegation Status Update with sub results
   106→    Parent->>Parent: Review, addWorkToSubAgent or synthesize
   107→    Parent->>User: Final response
   108→```
   109→
   110→- **Tools:** `delegateToSubAgent(name, instruction)` creates a sub-conversation and queues it. `addWorkToSubAgent(subConversationId, instruction)` sends follow-up work to a sub-agent after reviewing its output.
   111→- **Config:** `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`, `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds=30`. The model is instructed to delegate when its estimated time exceeds the threshold.
   112→
   113→## Features
   114→
   115→- **Multi-provider** -- OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama. Add/remove providers at runtime with API key persistence.
   116→- **Conversations** -- Stateful multi-turn sessions with full chat history, persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. The provider (agent) is selected **per prompt**: set or change it for the conversation so the next message uses that provider. Conversations have creation timestamps and can be sorted by time.
   117→- **Streaming** -- Conversation messages can be sent with `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` (SSE). Chunks include `reasoning`, `answer`, and `tool` types so the UI can show thinking and tools in real time.
   118→- **Delegation** -- Optional sub-agent delegation: the AI splits work into sub-conversations that run in parallel; when they complete, the parent is notified and can synthesize or request revisions. Enable with `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`.
   119→- **Thinking / reasoning** -- Supported for providers that expose it (e.g. Anthropic extended thinking). Reasoning is streamed separately and shown in the Web UI; scheduled job results include thinking in the stored output.
   120→- **Prompt caching** -- Conversation-history and prompt caching are used where supported (e.g. Anthropic, Gemini) to reduce cost and latency.
   121→- **Context window** -- Optional `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` (character limit) trims older messages and, for named bots, personal memory so the prompt fits. Use `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` (0–1) to split the budget between conversation history and bot personal memory. The AI can use the `retrieveOlderMessages` tool to fetch older conversation content when context is trimmed.
   122→- **Persistent memory** -- AI can save and recall information across sessions via `memory.json`.
   123→- **Skills** -- Drop instruction files into `~/.aidaemon/skills/` or install from [Smithery](https://smithery.ai) via REST endpoint. The AI reads them for domain-specific context.
   124→- **MCP support** -- Connect remote (Streamable HTTP, SSE) and local (stdio) MCP servers. Drop JSON configs into `~/.aidaemon/mcps/` and reload, or add [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCPs via chat (Notion, Google Calendar, etc.).
   125→- **Scheduled jobs** -- AI creates cron jobs (recurring or one-time) that autonomously execute instructions on schedule. AI can also list and cancel them.
   126→- **Shell access** -- Optionally allow the AI to execute host commands (git, docker, curl, etc.). Controlled by a global toggle endpoint.
   127→- **Web UI** -- React app in `frontend/webui/` for providers, conversations, and streaming chat with reasoning and tool visibility.
   128→- **Interactive CLI** -- Terminal-based chat with provider selection, conversation management, and shell toggle.
   129→
   130→## Prerequisites
   131→
   132→- Java 25+
   133→- Maven (or use the included `mvnw` wrapper)
   134→
   135→## Quick Start
   136→
   137→```bash
   138→# Clone and build
   139→git clone <repo-url>
   140→cd aidaemon
   141→./mvnw spring-boot:run
   142→```
   143→
   144→On Windows PowerShell:
   145→```powershell
   146→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run
   147→```
   148→
   149→The server starts on `http://localhost:8080`.
   150→
   151→### 1. Register a Provider
   152→
   153→```bash
   154→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   155→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   156→  -d '{"name":"my-gpt","type":"OPENAI","apiKey":"...","model":"gpt-4o"}'
   157→```
   158→
   159→Supported types: `OPENAI`, `ANTHROPIC`, `GEMINI`, `OLLAMA`
   160→
   161→For Ollama (no API key needed):
   162→```bash
   163→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   164→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   165→  -d '{"name":"local-llama","type":"OLLAMA","baseUrl":"http://localhost:11434","model":"llama3"}'
   166→```
   167→
   168→### 2. Chat (Stateless)
   169→
   170→```bash
   171→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/chat/{providerId} \
   172→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   173→  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'
   174→```
   175→
   176→### 3. Conversations (Stateful)
   177→
   178→Conversations are persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. Each has an optional name. The provider is selected per prompt: set it when creating or via `PATCH`; the next message you send will use that provider.
   179→
   180→```bash
   181→# Create a conversation
   182→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations \
   183→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   184→  -d '{"name":"My chat","providerId":"<id>"}'
   185→
   186→# Send messages (history is maintained server-side)
   187→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages \
   188→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   189→  -d '{"message":"What did I just ask you?"}'
   190→
   191→# Stream response (SSE: reasoning, answer, tool chunks)
   192→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream \
   193→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   194→  -d '{"message":"Explain X"}' \
   195→  -N
   196→
   197→# Set which provider to use for the next prompt(s)
   198→curl -X PATCH http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id} \
   199→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   200→  -d '{"providerId":"other-provider-id"}'
   201→```
   202→
   203→### 4. Interactive CLI
   204→
   205→```bash
   206→./mvnw spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli
   207→```
   208→
   209→On Windows PowerShell:
   210→```powershell
   211→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run "-Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli"
   212→```
   213→
   214→Commands inside CLI: `/quit`, `/new` (new conversation), `/shell` (toggle shell access).
   215→
   216→### 5. Web UI
   217→
   218→From the project root, build and run the React UI (proxy to the backend):
   219→
   220→```bash
   221→cd frontend/webui
   222→npm install
   223→npm run dev
   224→```
   225→
   226→Connect to the URL shown (e.g. Vite dev server). Configure providers and conversations, then chat; responses stream with reasoning and tool calls when available.
   227→
   228→## Skills
   229→
   230→Skills are folders containing a `SKILL.md` and optional resources (scripts, references, assets) that give the AI domain-specific knowledge.
   231→
   232→**Manual install:** Drop a skill folder into `~/.aidaemon/skills/`. With Smithery MCP enabled, ask the AI to search and install skills (e.g. "install the skill-creator skill from Smithery"); it uses `searchSmitherySkills` and `installSmitherySkill`.
   233→
   234→**Manage skills:** Ask the AI to list skills (`listSkills`), list or read files in a skill (`listSkillFiles`, `readSkillFile`), or remove a skill (`removeSkill`).
   235→
   236→## MCP Servers
   237→
   238→Create JSON config files in `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`.
   239→
   240→**Remote (Streamable HTTP):**
   241→```json
   242→{
   243→  "name": "github",
   244→  "type": "REMOTE",
   245→  "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
   246→  "headers": {
   247→    "Authorization": "Bearer <token>"
   248→  }
   249→}
   250→```
   251→
   252→**Remote (Legacy SSE):**
   253→```json
   254→{
   255→  "name": "my-sse-server",
   256→  "type": "SSE",
   257→  "url": "https://example.com/mcp/sse"
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→**Local (stdio):**
   262→```json
   263→{
   264→  "name": "aviation",
   265→  "type": "LOCAL",
   266→  "command": "npx",
   267→  "args": ["-y", "aviationstack-mcp-server"],
   268→  "env": {
   269→    "AVIATIONSTACK_API_KEY": "<key>"
   270→  }
   271→}
   272→```
   273→
   274→### Adding MCP via Smithery
   275→
   276→You can connect [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCP servers (e.g. Notion, Google Calendar) without writing config files by hand.
   277→
   278→1. **Enable and configure Smithery MCP** in `application.yaml` or environment:
   279→
   280→   ```yaml
   281→   aidaemon:
   282→     smithery-mcp:
   283→       enabled: true
   284→       namespace: <your-smithery-namespace>
   285→       api-key: <your-smithery-api-key>
   286→   ```
   287→
   288→   Get your namespace and API key from the [Smithery dashboard](https://smithery.ai).
   289→
   290→2. **Find a server** on [smithery.ai/servers](https://smithery.ai/servers) and note its slug (e.g. `notion`, `googlecalendar`) or full URL (e.g. `https://smithery.ai/servers/notion`).
   291→
   292→3. **Add the MCP via chat:** In any conversation, ask the AI to add the server by slug or URL, for example:
   293→   - “Add the Notion MCP from Smithery”
   294→   - “Add Smithery MCP server googlecalendar”
   295→
   296→   The AI uses the `addSmitheryMcp` tool, writes the config to `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`, and connects the server.
   297→
   298→4. **OAuth:** If the server requires sign-in, the tool returns an authorization URL. Open it in your browser, complete sign-in, then reload MCPs:
   299→
   300→   ```bash
   301→   curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   302→   ```
   303→
   304→```bash
   305→# List connected MCPs
   306→curl http://localhost:8080/api/mcps
   307→
   308→# Reload after adding/editing config files
   309→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   310→```
   311→
   312→## Shell Access
   313→
   314→Shell access is **disabled by default**. Toggle it at runtime:
   315→
   316→```bash
   317→# Enable
   318→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/enable
   319→
   320→# Disable
   321→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/disable
   322→
   323→# Check status
   324→curl http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access
   325→```
   326→
   327→When enabled, the AI can execute arbitrary commands on the host system.
   328→
   329→## Scheduled Jobs
   330→
   331→The AI can create cron jobs via tool calls. You can also manage them through REST:
   332→
   333→```bash
   334→# List all jobs
   335→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs
   336→
   337→# View execution results
   338→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/results
   339→
   340→# Cancel a job
   341→curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/{id}
   342→```
   343→
   344→## API Reference
   345→
   346→| Method | Endpoint | Description |
   347→|--------|----------|-------------|
   348→| `GET` | `/api/providers` | List registered providers |
   349→| `POST` | `/api/providers` | Register a new provider |
   350→| `DELETE` | `/api/providers/{id}` | Remove a provider |
   351→| `POST` | `/api/chat/{providerId}` | Stateless chat |
   352→| `POST` | `/api/conversations` | Create a conversation |
   353→| `PATCH` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Set provider for next prompt(s) (e.g. providerId) |
   354→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages` | Send a message |
   355→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` | Send a message (SSE stream) |
   356→| `GET` | `/api/conversations` | List conversations |
   357→| `DELETE` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Delete a conversation |
   358→| `GET` | `/api/mcps` | List connected MCP servers |
   359→| `POST` | `/api/mcps/reload` | Reload MCP configurations |
   360→| `GET` | `/api/shell-access` | Shell access status |
   361→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/enable` | Enable shell access |
   362→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/disable` | Disable shell access |
   363→| `GET` | `/api/jobs` | List scheduled jobs |
   364→| `GET` | `/api/jobs/results` | View job execution results |
   365→| `DELETE` | `/api/jobs/{id}` | Cancel a job |
   366→
   367→## Data Directory
   368→
   369→All persistent data lives in `~/.aidaemon/`:
   370→
   371→```
   372→~/.aidaemon/
   373→├── providers.json     # Registered LLM providers and API keys
   374→├── memory.json        # AI's persistent memory
   375→├── jobs.json          # Scheduled job definitions
   376→├── conversations/     # Conversation JSON files (stateful chats)
   377→├── skills/            # Skill folders (SKILL.md + resources)
   378→└── mcps/              # MCP server config JSON files
   379→```
   380→
   381→## Configuration
   382→
   383→Optional `application.yaml` / env properties:
   384→
   385→| Property | Default | Description |
   386→|----------|---------|-------------|
   387→| `aidaemon.config-dir` | `~/.aidaemon` | Data directory |
   388→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.enabled` | `false` | Enable adding MCP servers from Smithery via chat |
   389→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.namespace` | — | Smithery namespace (from dashboard) |
   390→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.api-key` | — | Smithery API key (from dashboard) |
   391→| `aidaemon.shell-access` | `false` | Allow AI shell execution |
   392→| `aidaemon.delegation-enabled` | `false` | Enable sub-agent delegation |
   393→| `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds` | `30` | Estimated seconds above which the model should delegate |
   394→| `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` | `0` | Max characters for conversation history (and, for named bots, conversation + personal memory combined). Use with `retrieveOlderMessages` for long chats |
   395→| `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` | `0` | For named bots only: share of chars-limit used for personal memory (0–1). Remainder is for conversation history |
   396→| `aidaemon.system-instructions` | (see application.yaml) | System prompt for the model |
   397→
   398→## Caution
   399→
   400→- **API keys are stored in plain text** in `~/.aidaemon/providers.json`. Protect this file accordingly.
   401→- **Shell access grants the AI full command execution** on your machine. Only enable it in trusted environments and disable it when not needed.
   402→- **Scheduled jobs run autonomously** using your configured provider and consume API tokens. Monitor your jobs and cancel any you no longer need.
   403→- **MCP servers may expose powerful capabilities** (file system access, API calls, etc.). Review MCP configurations before connecting them.
   404→- This project uses Spring AI milestone releases which may have breaking changes between versions.
   405→
   406→## Contributing
   407→
   408→Contributions are welcome! Whether it's bug fixes, new provider integrations, additional tools, or documentation improvements -- all PRs are appreciated.
   409→
   410→If you have ideas for new features or find issues, please open a GitHub issue to discuss before submitting a PR.
   411→

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report: AI Daemon

---

### 1. PROJECT OVERVIEW

**AI Daemon** is a **lightweight, self-hosted AI framework** built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. It serves as a unified interface for:
- Multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama)
- Multi-turn conversations with persistent memory
- Scheduled autonomous jobs
- Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for tool extensions
- Skills system for domain-specific knowledge
- Bot-to-bot messaging and agent delegation

**Type**: Spring Boot Java backend + React TypeScript frontend  
**Tech Stack**: Java 25, Spring Boot 4.0.3, React 19, Tailwind CSS, TypeScript  
**Build**: Maven with integrated frontend build (pom.xml orchestrates React build)

---

### 2. OVERALL PROJECT STRUCTURE

```
aidaemon/
├── frontend/webui/              # React UI (Node.js + Vite)
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── App.tsx              # Main app component (state management, routing)
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   │   ├── ChatWindow.tsx   # Chat UI, message display, input field
│   │   │   └── Sidebar.tsx      # Navigation, providers, bots, conversations
│   │   ├── api.ts               # REST API client (fetch wrapper)
│   │   ├── types.ts             # TypeScript interfaces
│   │   └── index.css            # Tailwind + custom styling
│   ├── vite.config.ts           # Vite config with backend proxy
│   └── package.json             # React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, TypeScript
│
├── src/main/java/               # Backend Spring Boot application
│   ├── chat/                    # Chat engine (messages, conversations, streaming)
│   ├── bot/                     # Bot system (named agents, personal memory, SOUL.md)
│   ├── provider/                # LLM provider registry (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
│   ├── web/                     # REST controllers (API endpoints)
│   ├── scheduling/              # Cron job executor
│   ├── skills/                  # Skills reader and installer
│   ├── mcp/                     # MCP server management
│   ├── delegation/              # Sub-agent delegation system
│   └── cli/                     # Terminal-based CLI
│
├── src/main/resources/
│   └── application.yaml         # Spring Boot configuration
│
├── pom.xml                      # Maven build config (orchestrates frontend build)
└── README.md                    # Documentation
```

---

### 3. FRONTEND/WEBUI - Detailed Analysis

#### **Technology Stack**
- **Framework**: React 19.2 with TypeScript 5.9
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS 4.2 + custom theme (dark mode)
- **Build**: Vite 7.3.1 with React SWC compiler
- **Backend Proxy**: Vite dev server proxies `/api` requests to `http://localhost:8080`

#### **Key Files**

**A. Main Application Component: `App.tsx`** (238 lines)
- **State Management**: 
  - Providers, bots, conversations, active conversation ID
  - Draft message input (ref-based to prevent re-renders)
  - Streaming state (`StreamingContent` with reasoning and parts)
  - Polling interval for updates after streaming completes
  
- **Key Responsibilities**:
  - Loads providers and bots on mount
  - Manages conversation lifecycle (create, select, delete)
  - Handles message sending and streaming
  - Implements fallback polling (2.5 second intervals, max 60 seconds) when streaming completes
  - Supports bot-to-bot conversations via `selectedBot` state

- **Message Flow**:
  ```typescript
  User input → handleSend() → sendMessageStream() → 
    onChunk callbacks → setStreaming() → 
    onDone() → polling → fetchConversations() → update UI
  ```

**B. Chat Window Component: `ChatWindow.tsx`** (372 lines)
- **Input Field**:
  - `<textarea>` at bottom with 2 rows (can expand)
  - Supports Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline
  - Disabled when `sending=true` or no provider selected
  - Shows placeholder: "Type a message… (Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline)"

- **Send Button**:
  - Located in fixed footer alongside dropdowns
  - Disabled states: `sending || !hasProvider || !inputDraft.trim()`
  - Direct call to `onSend(trimmed)` onclick

- **Message Display**:
  - `MessageEntry` component renders each message with:
    - Role label (USER, ASSISTANT, TOOL, or bot name)
    - Timestamp in HH:MM:SS.mmm format
    - Collapsible/expandable arrows
    - Content with whitespace-pre-wrap (preserves formatting)
  
- **Streaming Display**:
  - Reasoning content shown in `<details>` (collapsible, open by default)
  - Tool calls shown as separate parts with toggle buttons
  - Regular answer text concatenated inline
  - Last 120 chars of collapsed messages shown as preview

- **Dropdowns**:
  - **Agent Selector**: `<select>` for providers (required to send)
  - **Bot Selector**: Another `<select>` for named bots (only for non-direct chats)
  - Helper text: "Select agent to send" if no provider chosen

- **Additional Features**:
  - "Hide tools and thinking" toggle in header
  - Scroll-to-top/scroll-to-bottom buttons (up/down arrows)
  - Smooth scrolling to latest message

**C. Sidebar Component: `Sidebar.tsx`** (475 lines)
- **Top Section**:
  - "Add Provider" button with inline form:
    - Name, Type (OPENAI/ANTHROPIC/OLLAMA/GEMINI), API Key, Base URL, Model
    - Shows registered providers as badges
  - "Add Bot" button with form:
    - Bot name, Soul description (SOUL.md content)
    - Shows registered bots as badges
  
- **Navigation**:
  - "User" button (selects `selectedBot = null`)
  - "Bots" section: list of named bots as clickable buttons
  - "Conversations" section: tree view of conversations (parent-child hierarchy)
  
- **Conversation Tree**:
  - Sorted by creation time (newest first)
  - Collapse/expand arrows for parent conversations
  - Shows message count (numeric badge)
  - Delete button (× icon, with confirm dialog)
  - Shows last 4 messages when expanded (truncated preview)
  
- **Scheduled Jobs Section**:
  - Appears only when `selectedBot == null`
  - Shows conversations prefixed with `[SJ]` (scheduled job runs)

**D. API Client: `api.ts`** (158 lines)
- **Provider Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/providers` → fetch all
  - `POST /api/providers` → create (requires name, type, apiKey)
  - `DELETE /api/providers/{id}` → remove

- **Conversation Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/conversations?participant=X` → list (optional participant filter)
  - `POST /api/conversations` → create (name, providerId, optional botName)
  - `PATCH /api/conversations/{id}` → update (providerId, botName)
  - `DELETE /api/conversations/{id}` → delete
  - `GET /api/conversations/{id}` → get single
  - `GET /api/conversations/direct/{botName}` → get or create direct chat

- **Message Endpoints**:
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages` → send and wait (non-streaming)
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` → SSE streaming
    - Produces `MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE`
    - Returns `StreamChunk` objects with type and content
    - Client parses SSE format: `data: {JSON}`

- **Bot Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/bots` → list all
  - `POST /api/bots` → create (name, soul)

- **Streaming Parser**:
  ```typescript
  sendMessageStream(conversationId, message, onChunk, onDone, onError):
    Fetch SSE stream → parse "data: " lines → JSON.parse → 
    call onChunk(StreamChunk) → call onDone() on complete
  ```

**E. Type Definitions: `types.ts`** (43 lines)
```typescript
interface ChatMessage {
  role: string;
  content: string;
  timestampMillis?: number;
}

interface Conversation {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  providerId: string | null;
  botName?: string | null;
  messages: ChatMessage[];
  parentConversationId?: string | null;
  createdAtMillis?: number | null;
  participant1?: string | null;      // For bot-to-bot
  participant2?: string | null;      // For bot-to-bot
}

interface Provider {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  type: 'OPENAI' | 'ANTHROPIC' | 'OLLAMA' | 'GEMINI';
  baseUrl: string;
  model: string;
}

interface Bot {
  name: string;
}
```

**F. Styling: `index.css`** (65 lines)
- **Theme Variables** (dark mode):
  - Background: `#1a1b1e` (main), `#141517` (sidebar)
  - Text: `#c1c2c5` (normal), `#e9ecef` (bright), `#909296` (dim)
  - Accent: `#7c5cfc` (purple), `#6741f5` (hover)
  - User messages: `#2b2d5e` (blue background)
  - Tool output: `#1e2a1e` (green background with border)
  - Radius: 8px
- **Custom Scrollbar**: Thin gray scrollbar
- **Message Tool Parts**: Collapsible details styling

#### **Chat/Messaging UI Flow**

1. **User Types Message** → stored in `inputDraftRef.current`
2. **User Hits Enter or Clicks Send Button**
   - Trim message, add to UI immediately (optimistic)
   - Call `onSend(message)` from ChatWindow
3. **App.handleSend()**
   - Clear draft, set `sending=true`, initialize `streaming` state
   - Call `api.sendMessageStream()`
4. **Streaming Response**
   - Backend sends SSE chunks with type ('reasoning', 'answer', 'tool')
   - Frontend accumulates in `streaming` state
   - UI re-renders live (thinking shown in details, tools in collapsible, answers inline)
5. **Stream Complete**
   - `onDone()` called
   - Merge streamed content into last conversation message
   - Start polling with 2.5s intervals (max 60s)
   - Fetch full conversation from backend to get final state

---

### 4. AUDIO/MEDIA HANDLING - CURRENT STATUS

**Finding**: There is **NO audio/media handling code** in the current codebase.

- ✗ No voice input support
- ✗ No speech-to-text integration
- ✗ No audio file upload/playback
- ✗ No multipart form data handling for files
- ✗ No WebRTC or audio APIs
- ✗ Frontend: No `<audio>`, `<input type="file">`, or media elements
- ✗ Backend: No file upload endpoints, no base64 encoding for media
- ✗ No mention of "audio", "voice", "speech", "mp3", "wav" anywhere

**Message Content**: Currently text-only (string), stored in `ChatMessage.content` as plain strings.

---

### 5. BACKEND STRUCTURE - REST API & Message Flow

#### **Key Backend Files**

**A. REST Controllers**

**ConversationController.java** (`/api/conversations`)
```java
POST   /api/conversations              // create(name, providerId, botName)
PATCH  /api/conversations/{id}         // update(providerId, botName)
POST   /api/conversations/{id}/messages          // sendMessage(message)
POST   /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream  // sendMessageStream(message) [SSE]
GET    /api/conversations              // listAll() or listForParticipant()
GET    /api/conversations/{id}         // get(id)
DELETE /api/conversations/{id}         // delete(id)
GET    /api/conversations/direct/{botName}      // getOrCreateDirect(botName, providerId)
```

**Key Detail**: Stream endpoint returns `SseEmitter` with 300-second timeout, emitting `StreamChunk` objects (type + content).

**ChatController.java** (`/api/chat/{providerId}`)
- Stateless chat: `POST /api/chat/{providerId}` (single request/response)

**ProviderController.java** (`/api/providers`)
- CRUD for LLM provider registration (name, type, apiKey, model, baseUrl)

**BotController.java** (`/api/bots`)
- `POST /api/bots` → create bot (name, soul description)
- `GET /api/bots` → list

**Conversation Model**: `Conversation.java`
```java
record Conversation(
  String id,
  String name,
  String providerId,
  String botName,
  List<ChatMessage> messages,      // All conversation history
  String parentConversationId,     // For nested conversations
  Long createdAtMillis,
  String participant1,             // Bot-to-bot: first participant
  String participant2,             // Bot-to-bot: second participant
  Boolean direct                   // Bot-to-bot flag
)
```

**Chat Message Model**: `ChatMessage.java`
```java
record ChatMessage(
  String role,           // "user" or "assistant"
  String content,        // Text message content
  long timestampMillis   // Message timestamp
)
```

**B. Service Layer**

**ConversationService.java**
- `create(name, providerId, botName, parentConversationId)` → creates Conversation with UUID
- `sendMessage(conversationId, userMessage)` → 
  1. Adds user message to conversation
  2. Calls bot.chat() with all messages
  3. Adds assistant response
  4. Persists conversation
  5. Handles delegation if any sub-agents were spawned
  
- `sendMessageStream(conversationId, userMessage)` → returns `Flux<StreamChunk>`
  1. Same as sendMessage but returns reactive stream
  2. Backend streams reasoning, tool calls, and answer chunks in real-time
  3. Subscribes to bot.chatStream()

**ChatService.java** (100+ lines sampled)
- `stream(providerId, contextMessages, metadata, onComplete)` → 
  - Builds tool callbacks from all sources (skills, MCP, delegation)
  - Creates streaming chat model
  - Accumulates reasoning and content separately
  - Returns `Flux<StreamChunk>` with reasoning and answer parts
  - On complete, assembles structured JSON: `{"parts": [{type, content}]}`

**BotService.java**
- `getBot(name)` → returns Bot instance
- `listBots()` → all named bots
- `create(name, soul)` → creates bot directory with SOUL.md
- `loadSoul(name)` → reads SOUL.md content
- `loadPersonalMemory(name)` → loads bot's personal memory entries

**C. Bot-to-Bot Messaging** (Recent feature: "Fix bot to bot messaging")

From `ConversationService.sendMessageBotToBot()`:
```java
sendMessageBotToBot(callerBotName, targetBotName, message, providerId):
  - Validate both bots exist and are different
  - Get or create direct conversation with canonical ID
  - Send message via bot.chat()
```

**Direct Conversations**:
- ID: `canonicalId(participant1, participant2)` (sorted alphabetically)
- `participant1`, `participant2` fields track both parties
- `direct: true` flag
- UI filters to show conversations by `selectedBot`

**D. Data Persistence**

**ConversationRepository.java**
- Saves/loads from `~/.aidaemon/conversations/` as JSON files
- One JSON file per conversation (named by UUID)

All conversations in memory, persisted to JSON on disk after each message.

---

### 6. STREAMING & MESSAGE FLOW (End-to-End)

**Frontend → Backend**:
```
ChatWindow.onSend(message)
  ↓
App.handleSend(message)
  - Optimistically add user message to conversation
  - Set sending=true, initialize streaming state
  ↓
api.sendMessageStream(conversationId, message)
  - POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream
  - Body: { message: "..." }
  ↓
ConversationController.sendMessageStream()
  - Returns SseEmitter
  ↓
ConversationService.sendMessageStream()
  - Returns Flux<StreamChunk>
  ↓
ChatService.stream()
  - Gets chat model (streaming capable)
  - Calls chatModel.stream(prompt)
  - Emits chunks with type ('reasoning', 'answer', 'tool')
```

**Backend → Frontend (SSE)**:
```
Server sends (Server-Sent Events):
  data: {"type":"reasoning","content":"..."}
  
  data: {"type":"answer","content":"Hello..."}
  
  data: {"type":"tool","content":"..."}

Frontend receives:
  - parseSSE: extract "data: " lines
  - JSON.parse each line
  - Call onChunk callback
  - Accumulate in streaming state
  - Re-render ChatWindow in real-time
  
  On stream complete:
  - onDone() called
  - Save final message to conversation
  - Start polling for updates
```

**Streaming Content Structure**:
```typescript
interface StreamingContent {
  reasoning: string;         // Accumulated thinking/reasoning
  parts: StreamPart[];       // Array of {type, content} objects
}

enum StreamPartType {
  'reasoning',               // Extended thinking (if supported by provider)
  'answer',                  // Main response text
  'tool'                     // Tool call execution output
}
```

---

### 7. KEY FEATURES IDENTIFIED

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report: AI Daemon

---

### 1. PROJECT OVERVIEW

**AI Daemon** is a **lightweight, self-hosted AI framework** built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. It serves as a unified interface for:
- Multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama)
- Multi-turn conversations with persistent memory
- Scheduled autonomous jobs
- Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for tool extensions
- Skills system for domain-specific knowledge
- Bot-to-bot messaging and agent delegation

**Type**: Spring Boot Java backend + React TypeScript frontend  
**Tech Stack**: Java 25, Spring Boot 4.0.3, React 19, Tailwind CSS, TypeScript  
**Build**: Maven with integrated frontend build (pom.xml orchestrates React build)

---

### 2. OVERALL PROJECT STRUCTURE

```
aidaemon/
├── frontend/webui/              # React UI (Node.js + Vite)
│   ├── src/
│   │   ├── App.tsx              # Main app component (state management, routing)
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   │   ├── ChatWindow.tsx   # Chat UI, message display, input field
│   │   │   └── Sidebar.tsx      # Navigation, providers, bots, conversations
│   │   ├── api.ts               # REST API client (fetch wrapper)
│   │   ├── types.ts             # TypeScript interfaces
│   │   └── index.css            # Tailwind + custom styling
│   ├── vite.config.ts           # Vite config with backend proxy
│   └── package.json             # React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, TypeScript
│
├── src/main/java/               # Backend Spring Boot application
│   ├── chat/                    # Chat engine (messages, conversations, streaming)
│   ├── bot/                     # Bot system (named agents, personal memory, SOUL.md)
│   ├── provider/                # LLM provider registry (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
│   ├── web/                     # REST controllers (API endpoints)
│   ├── scheduling/              # Cron job executor
│   ├── skills/                  # Skills reader and installer
│   ├── mcp/                     # MCP server management
│   ├── delegation/              # Sub-agent delegation system
│   └── cli/                     # Terminal-based CLI
│
├── src/main/resources/
│   └── application.yaml         # Spring Boot configuration
│
├── pom.xml                      # Maven build config (orchestrates frontend build)
└── README.md                    # Documentation
```

---

### 3. FRONTEND/WEBUI - Detailed Analysis

#### **Technology Stack**
- **Framework**: React 19.2 with TypeScript 5.9
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS 4.2 + custom theme (dark mode)
- **Build**: Vite 7.3.1 with React SWC compiler
- **Backend Proxy**: Vite dev server proxies `/api` requests to `http://localhost:8080`

#### **Key Files**

**A. Main Application Component: `App.tsx`** (238 lines)
- **State Management**: 
  - Providers, bots, conversations, active conversation ID
  - Draft message input (ref-based to prevent re-renders)
  - Streaming state (`StreamingContent` with reasoning and parts)
  - Polling interval for updates after streaming completes
  
- **Key Responsibilities**:
  - Loads providers and bots on mount
  - Manages conversation lifecycle (create, select, delete)
  - Handles message sending and streaming
  - Implements fallback polling (2.5 second intervals, max 60 seconds) when streaming completes
  - Supports bot-to-bot conversations via `selectedBot` state

- **Message Flow**:
  ```typescript
  User input → handleSend() → sendMessageStream() → 
    onChunk callbacks → setStreaming() → 
    onDone() → polling → fetchConversations() → update UI
  ```

**B. Chat Window Component: `ChatWindow.tsx`** (372 lines)
- **Input Field**:
  - `<textarea>` at bottom with 2 rows (can expand)
  - Supports Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline
  - Disabled when `sending=true` or no provider selected
  - Shows placeholder: "Type a message… (Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline)"

- **Send Button**:
  - Located in fixed footer alongside dropdowns
  - Disabled states: `sending || !hasProvider || !inputDraft.trim()`
  - Direct call to `onSend(trimmed)` onclick

- **Message Display**:
  - `MessageEntry` component renders each message with:
    - Role label (USER, ASSISTANT, TOOL, or bot name)
    - Timestamp in HH:MM:SS.mmm format
    - Collapsible/expandable arrows
    - Content with whitespace-pre-wrap (preserves formatting)
  
- **Streaming Display**:
  - Reasoning content shown in `<details>` (collapsible, open by default)
  - Tool calls shown as separate parts with toggle buttons
  - Regular answer text concatenated inline
  - Last 120 chars of collapsed messages shown as preview

- **Dropdowns**:
  - **Agent Selector**: `<select>` for providers (required to send)
  - **Bot Selector**: Another `<select>` for named bots (only for non-direct chats)
  - Helper text: "Select agent to send" if no provider chosen

- **Additional Features**:
  - "Hide tools and thinking" toggle in header
  - Scroll-to-top/scroll-to-bottom buttons (up/down arrows)
  - Smooth scrolling to latest message

**C. Sidebar Component: `Sidebar.tsx`** (475 lines)
- **Top Section**:
  - "Add Provider" button with inline form:
    - Name, Type (OPENAI/ANTHROPIC/OLLAMA/GEMINI), API Key, Base URL, Model
    - Shows registered providers as badges
  - "Add Bot" button with form:
    - Bot name, Soul description (SOUL.md content)
    - Shows registered bots as badges
  
- **Navigation**:
  - "User" button (selects `selectedBot = null`)
  - "Bots" section: list of named bots as clickable buttons
  - "Conversations" section: tree view of conversations (parent-child hierarchy)
  
- **Conversation Tree**:
  - Sorted by creation time (newest first)
  - Collapse/expand arrows for parent conversations
  - Shows message count (numeric badge)
  - Delete button (× icon, with confirm dialog)
  - Shows last 4 messages when expanded (truncated preview)
  
- **Scheduled Jobs Section**:
  - Appears only when `selectedBot == null`
  - Shows conversations prefixed with `[SJ]` (scheduled job runs)

**D. API Client: `api.ts`** (158 lines)
- **Provider Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/providers` → fetch all
  - `POST /api/providers` → create (requires name, type, apiKey)
  - `DELETE /api/providers/{id}` → remove

- **Conversation Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/conversations?participant=X` → list (optional participant filter)
  - `POST /api/conversations` → create (name, providerId, optional botName)
  - `PATCH /api/conversations/{id}` → update (providerId, botName)
  - `DELETE /api/conversations/{id}` → delete
  - `GET /api/conversations/{id}` → get single
  - `GET /api/conversations/direct/{botName}` → get or create direct chat

- **Message Endpoints**:
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages` → send and wait (non-streaming)
  - `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` → SSE streaming
    - Produces `MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE`
    - Returns `StreamChunk` objects with type and content
    - Client parses SSE format: `data: {JSON}`

- **Bot Endpoints**:
  - `GET /api/bots` → list all
  - `POST /api/bots` → create (name, soul)

- **Streaming Parser**:
  ```typescript
  sendMessageStream(conversationId, message, onChunk, onDone, onError):
    Fetch SSE stream → parse "data: " lines → JSON.parse → 
    call onChunk(StreamChunk) → call onDone() on complete
  ```

**E. Type Definitions: `types.ts`** (43 lines)
```typescript
interface ChatMessage {
  role: string;
  content: string;
  timestampMillis?: number;
}

interface Conversation {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  providerId: string | null;
  botName?: string | null;
  messages: ChatMessage[];
  parentConversationId?: string | null;
  createdAtMillis?: number | null;
  participant1?: string | null;      // For bot-to-bot
  participant2?: string | null;      // For bot-to-bot
}

interface Provider {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  type: 'OPENAI' | 'ANTHROPIC' | 'OLLAMA' | 'GEMINI';
  baseUrl: string;
  model: string;
}

interface Bot {
  name: string;
}
```

**F. Styling: `index.css`** (65 lines)
- **Theme Variables** (dark mode):
  - Background: `#1a1b1e` (main), `#141517` (sidebar)
  - Text: `#c1c2c5` (normal), `#e9ecef` (bright), `#909296` (dim)
  - Accent: `#7c5cfc` (purple), `#6741f5` (hover)
  - User messages: `#2b2d5e` (blue background)
  - Tool output: `#1e2a1e` (green background with border)
  - Radius: 8px
- **Custom Scrollbar**: Thin gray scrollbar
- **Message Tool Parts**: Collapsible details styling

#### **Chat/Messaging UI Flow**

1. **User Types Message** → stored in `inputDraftRef.current`
2. **User Hits Enter or Clicks Send Button**
   - Trim message, add to UI immediately (optimistic)
   - Call `onSend(message)` from ChatWindow
3. **App.handleSend()**
   - Clear draft, set `sending=true`, initialize `streaming` state
   - Call `api.sendMessageStream()`
4. **Streaming Response**
   - Backend sends SSE chunks with type ('reasoning', 'answer', 'tool')
   - Frontend accumulates in `streaming` state
   - UI re-renders live (thinking shown in details, tools in collapsible, answers inline)
5. **Stream Complete**
   - `onDone()` called
   - Merge streamed content into last conversation message
   - Start polling with 2.5s intervals (max 60s)
   - Fetch full conversation from backend to get final state

---

### 4. AUDIO/MEDIA HANDLING - CURRENT STATUS

**Finding**: There is **NO audio/media handling code** in the current codebase.

- ✗ No voice input support
- ✗ No speech-to-text integration
- ✗ No audio file upload/playback
- ✗ No multipart form data handling for files
- ✗ No WebRTC or audio APIs
- ✗ Frontend: No `<audio>`, `<input type="file">`, or media elements
- ✗ Backend: No file upload endpoints, no base64 encoding for media
- ✗ No mention of "audio", "voice", "speech", "mp3", "wav" anywhere

**Message Content**: Currently text-only (string), stored in `ChatMessage.content` as plain strings.

---

### 5. BACKEND STRUCTURE - REST API & Message Flow

#### **Key Backend Files**

**A. REST Controllers**

**ConversationController.java** (`/api/conversations`)
```java
POST   /api/conversations              // create(name, providerId, botName)
PATCH  /api/conversations/{id}         // update(providerId, botName)
POST   /api/conversations/{id}/messages          // sendMessage(message)
POST   /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream  // sendMessageStream(message) [SSE]
GET    /api/conversations              // listAll() or listForParticipant()
GET    /api/conversations/{id}         // get(id)
DELETE /api/conversations/{id}         // delete(id)
GET    /api/conversations/direct/{botName}      // getOrCreateDirect(botName, providerId)
```

**Key Detail**: Stream endpoint returns `SseEmitter` with 300-second timeout, emitting `StreamChunk` objects (type + content).

**ChatController.java** (`/api/chat/{providerId}`)
- Stateless chat: `POST /api/chat/{providerId}` (single request/response)

**ProviderController.java** (`/api/providers`)
- CRUD for LLM provider registration (name, type, apiKey, model, baseUrl)

**BotController.java** (`/api/bots`)
- `POST /api/bots` → create bot (name, soul description)
- `GET /api/bots` → list

**Conversation Model**: `Conversation.java`
```java
record Conversation(
  String id,
  String name,
  String providerId,
  String botName,
  List<ChatMessage> messages,      // All conversation history
  String parentConversationId,     // For nested conversations
  Long createdAtMillis,
  String participant1,             // Bot-to-bot: first participant
  String participant2,             // Bot-to-bot: second participant
  Boolean direct                   // Bot-to-bot flag
)
```

**Chat Message Model**: `ChatMessage.java`
```java
record ChatMessage(
  String role,           // "user" or "assistant"
  String content,        // Text message content
  long timestampMillis   // Message timestamp
)
```

**B. Service Layer**

**ConversationService.java**
- `create(name, providerId, botName, parentConversationId)` → creates Conversation with UUID
- `sendMessage(conversationId, userMessage)` → 
  1. Adds user message to conversation
  2. Calls bot.chat() with all messages
  3. Adds assistant response
  4. Persists conversation
  5. Handles delegation if any sub-agents were spawned
  
- `sendMessageStream(conversationId, userMessage)` → returns `Flux<StreamChunk>`
  1. Same as sendMessage but returns reactive stream
  2. Backend streams reasoning, tool calls, and answer chunks in real-time
  3. Subscribes to bot.chatStream()

**ChatService.java** (100+ lines sampled)
- `stream(providerId, contextMessages, metadata, onComplete)` → 
  - Builds tool callbacks from all sources (skills, MCP, delegation)
  - Creates streaming chat model
  - Accumulates reasoning and content separately
  - Returns `Flux<StreamChunk>` with reasoning and answer parts
  - On complete, assembles structured JSON: `{"parts": [{type, content}]}`

**BotService.java**
- `getBot(name)` → returns Bot instance
- `listBots()` → all named bots
- `create(name, soul)` → creates bot directory with SOUL.md
- `loadSoul(name)` → reads SOUL.md content
- `loadPersonalMemory(name)` → loads bot's personal memory entries

**C. Bot-to-Bot Messaging** (Recent feature: "Fix bot to bot messaging")

From `ConversationService.sendMessageBotToBot()`:
```java
sendMessageBotToBot(callerBotName, targetBotName, message, providerId):
  - Validate both bots exist and are different
  - Get or create direct conversation with canonical ID
  - Send message via bot.chat()
```

**Direct Conversations**:
- ID: `canonicalId(participant1, participant2)` (sorted alphabetically)
- `participant1`, `participant2` fields track both parties
- `direct: true` flag
- UI filters to show conversations by `selectedBot`

**D. Data Persistence**

**ConversationRepository.java**
- Saves/loads from `~/.aidaemon/conversations/` as JSON files
- One JSON file per conversation (named by UUID)

All conversations in memory, persisted to JSON on disk after each message.

---

### 6. STREAMING & MESSAGE FLOW (End-to-End)

**Frontend → Backend**:
```
ChatWindow.onSend(message)
  ↓
App.handleSend(message)
  - Optimistically add user message to conversation
  - Set sending=true, initialize streaming state
  ↓
api.sendMessageStream(conversationId, message)
  - POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream
  - Body: { message: "..." }
  ↓
ConversationController.sendMessageStream()
  - Returns SseEmitter
  ↓
ConversationService.sendMessageStream()
  - Returns Flux<StreamChunk>
  ↓
ChatService.stream()
  - Gets chat model (streaming capable)
  - Calls chatModel.stream(prompt)
  - Emits chunks with type ('reasoning', 'answer', 'tool')
```

**Backend → Frontend (SSE)**:
```
Server sends (Server-Sent Events):
  data: {"type":"reasoning","content":"..."}
  
  data: {"type":"answer","content":"Hello..."}
  
  data: {"type":"tool","content":"..."}

Frontend receives:
  - parseSSE: extract "data: " lines
  - JSON.parse each line
  - Call onChunk callback
  - Accumulate in streaming state
  - Re-render ChatWindow in real-time
  
  On stream complete:
  - onDone() called
  - Save final message to conversation
  - Start polling for updates
```

**Streaming Content Structure**:
```typescript
interface StreamingContent {
  reasoning: string;         // Accumulated thinking/reasoning
  parts: StreamPart[];       // Array of {type, content} objects
}

enum StreamPartType {
  'reasoning',               // Extended thinking (if supported by provider)
  'answer',                  // Main response text
  'tool'                     // Tool call execution output
}
```

The build passes and changes are committed. Here's the summary:

| What | Why | How |

- Speaker button highlights in accent color when active
- Speaker click propagates stopped (won't accidentally collapse/expand the message)

- Both features degrade gracefully — unsupported browsers get an alert for mic; TTS is universally supported

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format

Perfect! Now let me compile a comprehensive report. I have all the necessary information. Let me create a detailed summary document:

---

## Comprehensive Codebase Analysis Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the aidaemon codebase, here's a detailed analysis of the tool calling, streaming, and configuration architecture:

### 1. TOOL CALLS IN CHATSERVICE.JAVA - FULL FLOW

**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\java\com\programmersdiary\aidaemon\chat\ChatService.java`

**Tool Registration:**
- Lines 67-79: Tools are registered via `LoggingToolCallback` wrappers
- `buildToolCallbacks(meta, config.id())` from `ChatToolCallbacksService` provides base tools
- MCP service tools are fetched via `mcpService.getToolCallbacksByServer()`
- Each tool is wrapped with `LoggingToolCallback` to track execution and emit streaming updates

**Tool Execution Flow:**
1. Tool callbacks are wrapped in `LoggingToolCallback` instances (lines 67-71)
2. Tools are passed to `chatModelFactory.create()` to build a model with tool support
3. During streaming (lines 95-108), Spring AI's streaming model automatically calls tools as needed
4. When a tool is invoked, `LoggingToolCallback.call()` is triggered (LoggingToolCallback lines 58-69)
5. Results are logged to `toolLog` list and emitted as `StreamChunk(TYPE_TOOL, content)` (line 77)

**Results Flow Back:**
- Tool execution results flow through the delegate callback pattern
- `onToolChunk` consumer (line 65) emits tool chunks to the sink (line 77 in LoggingToolCallback)
- Tool chunks merge into the main stream via `Flux.merge()` (line 110)
- Final ordered chunks are coalesced in `coalesceOrderedChunks()` (line 120)

---

### 2. STREAMING ARCHITECTURE

**StreamChunk Emission Points:**

**In ChatService.java:**
- Lines 87: Error responses as `StreamChunk(TYPE_ANSWER, error)`
- Lines 97-98: Content stream from model responses via `toStreamChunk()`
- Lines 101-105: Reasoning chunks accumulated
- Line 124: Reasoning chunks added to final result
- Line 64-65: Tool chunks emitted via onToolChunk consumer

**Stream Types:**
1. `TYPE_REASONING` - AI thinking/reasoning content
2. `TYPE_ANSWER` - AI response text
3. `TYPE_TOOL` - Tool execution input/output

**Streaming Merge Strategy:**
- Lines 95-108: Content stream from model
- Line 110: Merged with tool emission sink
- Line 116-130: Complete pipeline with ordering logic

**In ConversationService.java:**
- Lines 132-153: `sendMessageStream()` returns `Flux<StreamChunk>`
- Delegates to `bot.chatStream()` which calls `ChatService.stream()`
- onComplete callback handles conversation persistence and delegation

---

### 3. STREAMCHUNK CLASS

**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\java\com\programmersdiary\aidaemon\chat\StreamChunk.java`

```java
public record StreamChunk(String type, String content) {
    public static final String TYPE_REASONING = "reasoning";
    public static final String TYPE_ANSWER = "answer";
    public static final String TYPE_TOOL = "tool";
}
```

**Fields:**
- `type`: String - one of three enum-like constants (reasoning, answer, tool)
- `content`: String - the actual chunk content/text

---

### 4. SPRING AI TOOLCALLBACK / FUNCTIONCALLBACK INTEGRATION

**ToolCallback Usage:**
- `org.springframework.ai.tool.ToolCallback` is the Spring AI interface
- Located in `ChatToolCallbacksService` and wrapper classes
- Multiple implementations provided:
  - Built-in Spring AI tools from `ToolCallbacks.from()` conversion
  - MCP server tools
  - Custom tools (ChatTools, ShellTool, BotToBotTool, etc.)

**LoggingToolCallback Wrapper:**
**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\java\com\programmersdiary\aidaemon\chat\LoggingToolCallback.java`

- Decorator pattern wrapping Spring's `ToolCallback`
- Intercepts tool calls to:
  - Log execution to `toolLog` list (line 75)
  - Emit streaming chunks via `onToolChunk` consumer (lines 76-78)
  - Support tool name prefixing for MCP servers (line 42)
  - Sanitize tool names (line 48)

**Key Methods:**
- `call(String toolInput)` and `call(String toolInput, ToolContext toolContext)` - execute and log
- `getToolDefinition()` - provides tool metadata with optional server prefix
- `getToolMetadata()` - delegates to wrapped tool

---

### 5. APPLICATION.YAML CONFIGURATION

**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\resources\application.yaml`

**Relevant Config:**
```yaml
spring:
  application:
    name: aidaemon
  ai:
    mcp:
      client:
        enabled: false  # MCP client disabled (server-side uses smithery)

aidaemon:
  smithery-mcp:
    enabled: true
    api-key: ${SMITHERY_API_KEY}
    namespace: ${SMITHERY_NAMESPACE}
  shell-access: false
  delegation-enabled: false
  delegation-threshold-seconds: 30
  context-window:
    chars-limit: 5000
    personal-memory-ratio: 0.3
  config-dir: ${user.home}/.aidaemon
  system-instructions: |  # Instructions for bot behavior
    Use the saveMemory tool when the user asks you to remember something.
    Use listSkills to see installed skills, listSkillFiles to browse...
    [etc]
```

---

### 6. CONVERSATIONCONTROLLER.JAVA - SSE ENDPOINT

**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\web\ConversationController.java`

**SSE Endpoint (lines 84-100):**
```java
@PostMapping(value = "/{id}/messages/stream", produces = MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE)
public SseEmitter sendMessageStream(@PathVariable String id, @RequestBody Map<String, String> request) {
    var emitter = new SseEmitter(300_000L);  // 300 second timeout
    conversationService.sendMessageStream(id, request.get("message"))
        .subscribe(
            chunk -> {
                try {
                    emitter.send(SseEmitter.event().data(OBJECT_MAPPER.writeValueAsString(chunk)));
                } catch (IOException e) {
                    emitter.completeWithError(e);
                }
            },
            emitter::completeWithError,
            emitter::complete
        );
    return emitter;
}
```

**Key Details:**
- **Timeout:** 300,000 milliseconds (5 minutes)
- **Protocol:** Server-Sent Events (text/event-stream)
- **Encoding:** JSON serialization of `StreamChunk` objects
- **Subscription:** Flux<StreamChunk> subscribed with three callbacks (next, error, complete)

---

### 7. TOOL-RELATED CLASSES

**ChatToolCallbacksService.java** - Central tool registration
**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\java\com\programmersdiary\aidaemon\chat\ChatToolCallbacksService.java`

Lines 52-73: `buildToolCallbacks()` method registers:
1. **ChatTools** - Core skills (saveMemory, readMemory, listSkills, etc.)
2. **ShellTool** - Shell command execution (if enabled)
3. **BotToBotTool** - Inter-bot messaging
4. **SmitheryMcpTool** - Smithery MCP integration
5. **SmitherySkillTool** - Smithery skills
6. **MCP Server tools** - From `mcpService.getToolCallbacksByServer()`

**BotToBotTool.java** - Tool for inter-bot communication
**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\src\main\java\com\programmersdiary\aidaemon\chat\BotToBotTool.java`

Tools:
- `listOtherBots()` - Lists available bots (excluding self)
- `messageBot(targetBotName, message)` - Sends message to another bot

---

### 8. FRONTEND STREAMING HANDLING - CHATWINDOW.TSX

**File:** `C:\Users\evalvis\Desktop\Projects\AI\aidaemon\frontend\webui\src\components\ChatWindow.tsx`

**Streaming Chunk Processing (App.tsx lines 150-172):**
```typescript
api.sendMessageStream(
  activeId,
  message,
  (chunk) => {
    setStreaming((prev) => {
      if (chunk.type === 'reasoning') {
        next = { ...prev, reasoning: prev.reasoning + chunk.content };
      } else if (chunk.type === 'tool') {
        next = { ...prev, parts: [...prev.parts, { type: 'tool', content: chunk.content }] };
      } else {  // 'answer' type
        // Coalesce consecutive answer chunks
        const last = prevParts[prevParts.length - 1];
        if (last?.type === 'answer') {
          next = { ...prev, parts: [...prevParts.slice(0, -1), { type: 'answer', content: last.content + chunk.content }] };
        } else {
          next = { ...prev, parts: [...prevParts, { type: 'answer', content: chunk.content }] };
        }
      }
      return next;
    });
  },
  onDone,  // Called when stream completes
  onError
);
```

**Tool Chunk Display (ChatWindow.tsx lines 152-161):**
- Tool chunks rendered as collapsible `<details>` elements
- Content shown in monospace font with tool-specific styling
- Tool output displayed on-demand via expand/collapse UI

**Streaming Content Structure:**
```typescript
interface StreamingContent {
  reasoning: string;
  parts: StreamPart[];
}

interface StreamPart {
  type: 'answer' | 'tool' | 'reasoning';
  content: string;
}
```

**Frontend API (api.ts lines 83-139):**
- Uses `ReadableStream` from Fetch API
- Parses SSE events (format: `data: {...}\n\n`)
- JSON-decodes each chunk
- Handles streaming completion and errors

---

### 9. DATA FLOW ARCHITECTURE

**Request Flow:**
```
ConversationController.sendMessageStream()
  → ConversationService.sendMessageStream()
    → Bot.chatStream()
      → ChatService.stream()
        → StreamingChatModel.stream()
          + Tool invocation (via LoggingToolCallback)
            → LoggingToolCallback.call()
              → emit StreamChunk(TYPE_TOOL)
        → toStreamChunk() - converts responses
        → Flux.merge() - combines model + tool streams
        → SseEmitter.send(JSON) - sends to frontend
```

**Response Flow:**
```
Frontend receives SSE events
  → api.ts parses chunks
    → setStreaming(state) in App.tsx
      → ChatWindow displays dynamically
        → Tool chunks: collapsed details element
        → Answer chunks: appended text
        → Reasoning chunks: thinking details
```

---

### 10. KEY INTEGRATION POINTS

**Spring AI Integration:**
- Uses `org.springframework.ai.chat.model.StreamingChatModel`
- Uses `org.springframework.ai.tool.ToolCallback` interface
- Uses `org.springframework.ai.support.ToolCallbacks.from()` for annotation-based tool discovery
- Uses `org.springframework.ai.chat.model.ToolContext` for tool context

**Reactor Integration:**
- `Flux<StreamChunk>` for reactive streaming
- `Sinks.many().unicast()` for tool emission buffer
- Back-pressure handling via `onBackpressureBuffer()`

**Streaming Behavior:**
- Chunks streamed in real-time as they arrive

### 6. CONVERSATIONCONTROLLER.JAVA - SSE ENDPOINT

**SSE Endpoint (lines 84-100):**
```java
@PostMapping(value = "/{id}/messages/stream", produces = MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE)
public SseEmitter sendMessageStream(@PathVariable String id, @RequestBody Map<String, String> request) {
    var emitter = new SseEmitter(300_000L);  // 300 second timeout
    conversationService.sendMessageStream(id, request.get("message"))
        .subscribe(
            chunk -> {
                try {
                    emitter.send(SseEmitter.event().data(OBJECT_MAPPER.writeValueAsString(chunk)));
                } catch (IOException e) {
                    emitter.completeWithError(e);
                }
            },
            emitter::completeWithError,
            emitter::complete
        );
    return emitter;
}
```

**Key Details:**
- **Timeout:** 300,000 milliseconds (5 minutes)
- **Protocol:** Server-Sent Events (text/event-stream)
- **Encoding:** JSON serialization of `StreamChunk` objects
- **Subscription:** Flux<StreamChunk> subscribed with three callbacks (next, error, complete)

api.sendMessageStream(
  activeId,
  message,
  (chunk) => {
    setStreaming((prev) => {
      if (chunk.type === 'reasoning') {
        next = { ...prev, reasoning: prev.reasoning + chunk.content };
      } else if (chunk.type === 'tool') {
        next = { ...prev, parts: [...prev.parts, { type: 'tool', content: chunk.content }] };
      } else {  // 'answer' type
        // Coalesce consecutive answer chunks
        const last = prevParts[prevParts.length - 1];
        if (last?.type === 'answer') {
          next = { ...prev, parts: [...prevParts.slice(0, -1), { type: 'answer', content: last.content + chunk.content }] };
        } else {
          next = { ...prev, parts: [...prevParts, { type: 'answer', content: chunk.content }] };
        }
      }
      return next;
    });
  },
  onDone,  // Called when stream completes

);

content.text:
Committed as `47ae6e1`. Here's the summary:

| What | Why | How |
|------|-----|-----|
| `aidaemon.manual-approve` config | Toggle to enable/disable the feature | Added to `application.yaml` (currently `true`), read via `@Value` in `ChatService` and `ConversationController` |
| `ToolApprovalService` | Tracks pending tool approvals | `ConcurrentHashMap<approvalId, CompletableFuture<Boolean>>` — `approve()`/`reject()` complete the future |
| `ToolApprovalController` | REST endpoints for frontend to call | `POST /api/tools/{id}/approve` and `reject` — delegates to `ToolApprovalService` |
| `LoggingToolCallback` intercepts before calling delegate | Block execution until user decides | Registers future first, emits `tool_pending` chunk (with JSON `{approvalId, toolName, toolInput}`), then blocks on `future.get()`. On reject, returns rejection string without calling delegate |
| `SseEmitter(0L)` when manual-approve enabled | Prevent timeout while waiting | `ConversationController` uses no-timeout emitter so connections don't expire mid-approval |
| `ToolApprovalCard` in frontend | Approval UI | Shows tool name + auto-expanded input; Approve (accent) / Reject (danger) buttons. Card disappears after decision |
| `tool_pending` chunk handling in `App.tsx` | Drive approval state | Adds to `pendingApprovals[]`; when `tool` chunk follows, removes matching entry; cleared on stream complete |
| **9 tests** | TDD coverage | `ToolApprovalServiceTest` (6) + `LoggingToolCallbackApprovalTest` (3 — null service, approved, rejected) |

message.content: but what happens if after approval command execution gets stuck. Will it gain no expiration? It should not

content.text:
Good catch. `SseEmitter(0L)` removes timeout for the entire connection — so if a tool hangs *after* approval, it also waits forever. 

Fix: keep `SseEmitter(0L)` for the approval wait, but wrap `delegate.call()` with a configurable execution timeout using `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync().get(timeout)`.

I need to add a `toolExecutionTimeoutSeconds` parameter — the no-timeout only covers the approval wait, but execution after approval should have a finite limit. Let me update `LoggingToolCallback`, `ChatService`, `application.yaml`, and add a timeout test.
